name: Japan

notes: Direct and one-stop options; align travel with MVV validity and first-week appointments in the Netherlands.
Long-stay MVV collection and appointments are described on Netherlands Worldwide for Japan—confirm the Dutch embassy in Tokyo and current booking flow.

commonReasons: Sponsored roles with Dutch or European employers
Startup or entrepreneurship where criteria are met
Regional corporate transfers into Dutch entities

commonRoutes: Highly skilled migrant and other sponsored employment
Study with a Dutch institution as sponsor
Startup or self-employment where IND criteria are met
Intra-company or assignment structures where applicable
notes: Japan is outside the EU/EEA/Switzerland: long stays are permit-driven, and an MVV (long-stay entry visa, often described as a Type D national visa sticker) is required in many—but not all—procedures; verify the MVV exemption list and your specific route on the IND.
Short-stay Schengen rules are a different process from relocating for work, study, or family.

MVV and residence permit applications are often linked from abroad; the IND decision letter indicates where to collect the MVV sticker, commonly via the Dutch mission network in Japan (e.g. Tokyo) when that applies to you.

commonStarterDocuments: Birth, marriage, or civil documents when relevant to your route
Educational and employment evidence for study or work permits
Sponsor or admission documentation as required
countrySpecificNotes: For many Japan-issued documents used in the Netherlands, legalisation with a Hague apostille through the Japanese Ministry of Foreign Affairs (MOFA) is the standard path described on Netherlands Worldwide.
Sworn translations may be required when Dutch authorities request Dutch or English versions.
Netherlands Worldwide publishes Japan-specific legalisation guidance—cross-check each document type.

heroImageAlt: Calm daylight on a minimal Japanese-style interior: soft light through a translucent screen, a closed passport, suitcase handle, tea cup, and a single cherry blossom stem—editorial mood for planning a move from Japan to the Netherlands.

seo.title: Moving to the Netherlands from Japan | Visa, MVV, Documents & Practical Setup
seo.description: Planning a move from Japan to the Netherlands? Explore visa routes, MVV guidance, document apostille requirements, housing, and the practical steps to settle in.
keywords: moving to the netherlands from japan

customNotes: Japan-origin moves often hinge on MOFA apostille timing, embassy appointment slots, and MVV validity windows.

faqOverrides.q: Do Japanese nationals need a visa to visit the Netherlands?
faqOverrides.a: Short stays in the Schengen area follow Schengen short-stay rules. Netherlands Worldwide has Japan-specific pages for Schengen visas and entry visas when one is required. Many Japanese passport holders can visit short-term without a Schengen visa under current rules, but purpose of stay and edge cases still matter—and short visits are not the same as relocating for work or study.

faqOverrides.q: Do I need an MVV to move from Japan to the Netherlands?
faqOverrides.a: For stays longer than short visits, you normally need a residence permit for a recognised purpose. Many routes also require an MVV long-stay entry visa (a Type D visa sticker) before travel, but exemptions exist for some situations—check the IND’s MVV information and your specific permit type rather than assuming one answer for everyone.

faqOverrides.q: Where is the MVV collected when applying from Japan?
faqOverrides.a: After a positive decision, the IND letter states where to collect the MVV sticker. Netherlands Worldwide describes MVV applications and appointments for Japan; collection and appointments are often handled through the Dutch embassy in Tokyo when that applies to your procedure—always follow the current official instructions.

faqOverrides.q: How do I legalise Japanese documents for Dutch authorities?
faqOverrides.a: Many Japanese public documents need a Hague apostille through the Japanese Ministry of Foreign Affairs (MOFA) for use in the Netherlands, as explained on Netherlands Worldwide. You may still need sworn translation depending on the receiving body.

faqOverrides.q: What about the civic integration exam abroad?
faqOverrides.a: Depending on your route, civic integration requirements may apply. Netherlands Worldwide lists Japan-specific information for the civic integration exam abroad when relevant to your situation.

faqOverrides.q: What should I plan for immediately after arriving?
faqOverrides.a: Most people prioritise municipal registration and BSN, residence permit steps if applicable, a bank account, and Dutch basic health insurance when resident. DigiD and GP registration follow once identifiers exist.
No. This guide summarises common planning themes and links to official sources. Always confirm your situation with the IND, Netherlands Worldwide, your sponsor, or qualified advisors.

scenarioOverrides.title: Japanese professional relocating for a Dutch job
scenarioOverrides.summary: Coordinate sponsor timelines, MVV collection per your IND letter, housing within commute range, and BSN-first banking and insurance.

scenarioOverrides.title: Student moving from Japan to a Dutch university
scenarioOverrides.summary: Match admission to study permit conditions; plan MOFA apostilles on academic and civil documents; budget for student housing.

scenarioOverrides.title: Partner or family relocation from Japan
scenarioOverrides.summary: Relationship evidence and civil documents are central; timelines depend on the partner’s status in the Netherlands.

scenarioOverrides.title: Entrepreneur exploring startup or self-employment
scenarioOverrides.summary: Map IND criteria, possible facilitator involvement, and municipality registration—expect more bespoke evidence than a standard employee route.

scenarioOverrides.title: Corporate transfer from a Japan office
scenarioOverrides.summary: Clarify sponsor, permit type, MVV sequencing, and payroll transition with your mobility team.
